flashcards.back: The fundamental compute building block of an NVIDIA GPU, containing CUDA cores, Tensor Cores, SFUs, load/store units, warp schedulers, a register file, and shared memory. Thread blocks are assigned to SMs for execution.

Specialized processing units (introduced in Volta) designed for matrix multiply-and-accumulate operations. They accelerate deep learning by performing mixed-precision matrix math (e.g., FP16 inputs with FP32 accumulation).

flashcards.front: Describe the GPU memory hierarchy from fastest to slowest.
flashcards.back: Registers (per-thread) > Shared Memory (per-block) > L1 Cache (per-SM) > L2 Cache (chip-wide) > Global Memory (off-chip DRAM). Constant and texture memories have dedicated caches.

flashcards.front: What is warp divergence and why is it harmful?
flashcards.back: Warp divergence occurs when threads in a warp follow different branches of a conditional. The warp must serialize execution of each branch with the other threads masked off, reducing effective parallelism and throughput.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps on an SM to the maximum supported warps. It is limited by register usage, shared memory usage, and thread block size. Higher occupancy helps hide memory latency.
